subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Two-Year Limitation for Equalization Claim Under FLA s.7(3)
fact_pattern: Donna and Raymond separated in January 2021. They attempted reconciliation for several months, failing permanently in October 2021. Raymond never commenced divorce proceedings. In March 2026, Donna files an application for equalization of net family property under the Family Law Act. Raymond argues her claim is statute-barred under the FLA's specific limitation period.
call_of_question: What limitation period governs Donna's equalization claim under the Family Law Act, and when does it begin to run?

options.A: Donna's equalization claim is subject to the general two-year limitation under the Limitations Act, 2002, running from the date of separation in January 2021; her claim filed in March 2026 is statute-barred.
options.B: Under FLA s.7(3), a claim for equalization of net family property must be commenced within two years after the day the marriage is terminated by divorce or judgment of nullity, within two years after the day a separation agreement is entered into, or within six years after the day the spouses separate and there is no reasonable prospect of resumption of cohabitation; Donna's claim must be analyzed under these specific triggering events.
options.C: There is no limitation period for equalization claims in Ontario; they may be brought at any time during or after the marriage.
options.D: The limitation period for equalization is ten years from separation.

explanation: Family Law Act s.7(3) provides three specific limitation periods for equalization claims: (a) two years after the day the marriage is terminated by divorce or judgment of nullity; (b) two years after the day a separation agreement is entered into; or (c) six years after the day the spouses separate in circumstances that appear to be permanent. These are specific limitation periods under the FLA that displace the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year period. On the facts, if there is no divorce and no separation agreement, Donna must rely on the six-year period from the date of permanent separation (October 2021, when the reconciliation finally failed). If the permanent separation date is October 2021, the six-year period runs to October 2027, meaning Donna's March 2026 application is within time. Raymond's argument based on the January 2021 date may fail if the permanent separation date is found to be October 2021.
why_A_wrong: The general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year period does not govern equalization claims. FLA s.7(3) contains specific limitation periods that displace the general Act. The FLA's six-year period from permanent separation is the applicable limitation here.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.7(3) provides three specific limitation triggers — divorce, separation agreement, or six years from permanent separation; the general Limitations Act does not apply; Donna's claim may be in time if permanent separation is October 2021.
why_C_wrong: FLA s.7(3) expressly imposes limitation periods for equalization claims. The claim is not unlimited in time; the six-year period from permanent separation applies where there is no divorce or separation agreement.
why_D_wrong: There is no ten-year limitation for equalization claims. FLA s.7(3) provides a maximum of six years from permanent separation (absent divorce or separation agreement), not ten.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: Equalization Claim Limitation — FLA s.7(3)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 7(3)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Limitation Period for Matrimonial Home Possession Orders
subtopic_heading: Limitation Period for Exclusive Possession Application — FLA s.19
fact_pattern: Peter and Joan separated in 2020. Joan remained in the matrimonial home with the children. No exclusive possession order was ever sought. The home is registered in both names. Peter recently announced he plans to sell the home and wants Joan to vacate. It is now 2026. Joan consults a lawyer about whether she can now apply for exclusive possession.
call_of_question: Is Joan's potential application for exclusive possession of the matrimonial home subject to a limitation period, and what law governs?

options.A: There is no limitation period for exclusive possession applications; Joan may apply at any time regardless of how long the parties have been separated.
options.B: The general two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 applies to exclusive possession applications, running from the date Joan knew or ought to have known her right to possession was threatened; a court would assess when the claim was discovered.
options.C: The limitation period for exclusive possession is six years from the date of separation under FLA s.7(3).
options.D: Exclusive possession orders are not available more than one year after separation.

explanation: Unlike equalization claims, which have specific limitation periods under FLA s.7(3), there is no equivalent specific limitation period in the FLA for exclusive possession applications. Courts have applied the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation period to exclusive possession claims. The two-year period runs from the date the claim was discovered — that is, when Joan knew or ought to have known that: (a) she had a right to apply for exclusive possession; and (b) a proceeding was an appropriate means to enforce that right. The discovery date may be the date of separation, or it may be later — such as when Peter first threatened to sell the home or demanded Joan vacate. Joan should seek advice urgently as the limitation period may apply.
why_A_wrong: Exclusive possession applications are civil claims subject to limitation periods under the Limitations Act, 2002. They are not exempt from limitation periods. Joan's delay of 6 years raises serious limitation concerns.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic period applies to exclusive possession claims, running from discovery; Peter's recent threat to sell may extend the discovery date.
why_C_wrong: FLA s.7(3) applies specifically to equalization of net family property claims, not to exclusive possession applications. There is no equivalent six-year special period for possession claims.
why_D_wrong: There is no one-year cutoff for exclusive possession orders in the FLA. The applicable limitation is the general two-year period under the Limitations Act, 2002 from the date of discovery.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: Limitation Period — Exclusive Possession Applications
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 19; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4

tested_concepts: no specific FLA limitation for possession claims

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Limitation Periods for Spousal Support and Child Support Claims Compared
fact_pattern: Lucy and David separated in March 2021. Lucy did not apply for spousal support at the time. She also did not apply for child support for their three children, relying instead on informal arrangements. By March 2026, she consults a lawyer about both claims. The lawyer must advise whether both claims may be out of time.
call_of_question: What is the limitation period for a spousal support claim under the FLA compared to a child support claim under the Divorce Act or FCSG?

options.A: Both spousal support and child support claims under the FLA are subject to the same two-year limitation period with no exceptions.
options.B: Spousal support under the FLA is subject to the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation from discovery; however, child support claims benefit from special protection — under the FCSG, no limitation period restricts the court from making an initial child support order at any time, as the child's right to support is paramount and may not be waived by the delay of a parent.
options.C: Both spousal support and child support claims have no limitation period whatsoever; they may be brought at any time.
options.D: Child support is subject to a six-year limitation period; spousal support has no limitation period.

explanation: The limitation period framework for support claims in family law is asymmetric. Spousal support claims under FLA Part III are subject to the general Limitations Act, 2002 two-year basic limitation period from discovery. A claim to obtain an initial spousal support order may be time-barred if the claimant unreasonably delayed bringing the application. Child support, by contrast, is treated differently: courts have consistently held that delay by a custodial parent in seeking child support does not disentitle the child from support. Child support belongs to the child, not the parent, and a parent's failure to apply promptly does not bar an initial child support order. Lucy's child support claim can still be made; her spousal support claim faces a more serious limitation argument, depending on when her claim was discovered.
why_A_wrong: Child support and spousal support are not treated identically for limitation purposes. Child support benefits from a more protective approach due to the child's independent right to support; spousal support is subject to the general two-year Limitations Act period.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Spousal support faces a two-year limitation from discovery; child support is not limited by parental delay — the child's right to support cannot be extinguished by a parent's delay.
why_C_wrong: Spousal support claims under the FLA are subject to the Limitations Act, 2002 two-year period. They are not unlimited in time. Child support is more protected from limitation arguments, but the positions differ.
why_D_wrong: There is no six-year limitation period for child support. Child support claims benefit from a policy against limiting the child's right to support through parental delay — this is not a specific six-year limitation, but a broader policy of not extinguishing child support through limitation defences.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 11 — Limitation Periods in Family Law
source_reference.heading: Limitation Period — Spousal Support vs Child Support
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part III; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4; Federal Child Support Guidelines, SOR/97-175

tested_concepts: spousal support two-year limitation from discovery
child support not barred by parental delay
